title: Na'vi Vowels
objective: Learn the noun cases used in the Na'vi language.

data.text: Cases are the system in which a word changes form to indicate if they're a subject, object, or possesson. In Na'vi, this is done by changing the ending of a word
Na'vi has 6 cases: Subjective, Agentive, Patientive, Genitive, Dative, and Topic.

data.description: This case is used for subject of intransitive (not direct) verbs. There is no ending suffix.
This case is used for transitive (able to take a direct object) verbs. The ending forms are -l and ìl
This case is used to indicate the direct object of a transitive verb. The ending forms are -t, -it and -ti. You should never see this case in a sentence with an intransitive verb

data.translation: He gives a book to the viperwolf
data.description: This case is used to indicate the direct object of the sentence.  An indirect object describes 'to whom' or 'for whom' an action is performed. The endings are -r, -ru and -ur
This case is used to indicate possession,.  In Na'vi the genitive can come before or after the noun it possesses. The endings are -ä and -yä

data.translation: She thanks the viperwolf for the book
data.description: This case is a little tricky. You use it when talking about things that may fall under the category 'for X, concerning X'. When thanking someone for somtehing or apologizing to someone for something, the thing you're thanking/apologixng for goes in the topical. The endings are  -ri and -ìri

data.question: Which case do we use when indicating possesion
Which ending do we use for Dative
Which sentence usies the Agentive case

data.text: 🎉 Great work! You now know the six case!.
